You're becoming an expert level designer now. You  
can use includes to manage sharing assets between  
levels, you have features like activation ranges and  
TFB Tool  
Handbook  
tags to help get more control over your mechanics,  
and you can debug with the best of them via  
breakpoints and searching in scripts.  
There are a handful of advanced concepts that aren't  
Ch. 1: The TFB Way  
Ch. 2: Creating a Level  
required for getting things done but will aid in your  
scripting skill tremendously, especially when it comes  
to increasing the performance and maintainability of  
your scripts. In this chapter, we'll cover:  
Ch. 3: Basic Scripting

block

Set References

Ch. 7: Advanced Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._7:_Advanced_Scri...  
From the very end of the last section, we utilized a tag to track the enemy spawner's  
actors. If you remember, we made a performance decision by copying the contents out  
rather than referencing the tag directly every time we wanted to change the set, since  
ﬁnd variable is a little slow. Instead of copying the contents out upon startup, we could  
use a set reference, a reference that points to a set instead of an object, to point to the  
tag and just operate upon the reference.  
Open the Enemy Manager script and create a new actor *[set reference] in the  
STARTUP. Name it enemies ref.  
In the ﬁnd variable block in the startup, add a new set *reference command and  
point the *[enemies ref] set reference at the ^[enemies tag] set.  
Since you'll be deprecating the now unnecessary set, [enemies cloned], you should  
delete the associated create variable - then you can ﬁnd all the places you used it and  
update the line.  
Highlight the create variable line and delete it now.  
2 of 20  
01/11/2016 03:27 PM  
Ch. 7: Advanced Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._7:_Advanced_Scri...  
All usages of that variable should now turn red.  
Replace every red parameter to utilize the new *[enemies ref] set reference,  
except those in the STARTUP and SHUTDOWN.  
3 of 20  
01/11/2016 03:27 PM  
Ch. 7: Advanced Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._7:_Advanced_Scri...  
I didn't modify the set [set] lines in the startup and shutdown because they're not  
necessary anymore. Since we're referencing the tag directly, we don't need the lines that  
handle the adjusting of sets. We only have one set, the ^[enemies tag], and we  
manipulate it directly by way of the *[enemies ref] reference.  
Delete the set [set] lines within the ﬁnd variable commands in the STARTUP and  
SHUTDOWN.  
You may note that the ﬁnd variable in the SHUTDOWN actually has nothing in it, but  
before we delete it, there's one more thing we need to change. In the current version of  
the script, the [enemies tag] set is being created in the SHUTDOWN. If we were to run,  
right now, the ﬁnd variable in the STARTUP would fail, since the tag doesn't exist. That  
would be pretty bad, since the set *reference line for the *[enemies ref] variable would  
also fail. Our set reference would be null, and all the lines that operate on it would fail, as  
well. We need to create the tag in STARTUP, now, to make sure it's always there.  
Change the as tag parameter in the STARTUP ﬁnd variable to force tag.  
4 of 20  
01/11/2016 03:27 PM  
Ch. 7: Advanced Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._7:_Advanced_Scri...  
Delete the now-unnecessary ﬁnd variable in the SHUTDOWN. The ﬁnished script  
should look as follows.  
By using a *[set reference], we've signiﬁcantly reduced the complexity of the script and  
made it much simpler to debug and understand - there's only a single actor set (the tag),  
and we always operate on it, even though we may be going through a reference.  
5 of 20

http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._7:_Advanced_Scri...  
Loop Value  
In an earlier chapter, we learned about dealing with sets using the for each command to  
loop over them. For each is a very useful command, but it also has a hidden cost, since  
memory needs to be allocated for the scoped ^current in [for each] reference that it  
provides to you. It creates an entire copy of the set you do the for each upon, then runs  
through it. Now, this all happens in code, so it's not as slow as you doing the same thing  
in script, but allocating memory, as a general rule, is a slow thing to do. While you may  
use for each to run through a set, I ﬁnd a lot more speed and power in using the loop  
value command. Loop value, quite simply, says: run the inserted lines n times. While  
you can type in a constant to have something happen multiple times, it's particularly  
powerful use comes when looping over the count of a set. Let's change the Player  
Projectile's for each command to be a faster loop value.  
Open the Player Projectile's script and add a new loop value command right above  
the for each.  
Set the count parameter of the loop value to be the ^[subset]'s count.  
What the for each provides that the loop value does not is a reference to to the current  
member in the set as we loop over it (^current in [for each] in the case of for each).  
loop value does, however, provide a ^loop index which counts up from 0 every time the  
ﬂow loop value is reached. Some commands naturally let you index into a set if you point  
it to a set, like set *reference. What we'll want to do is create an actor *reference to  
use as a scratch variable which points into the ^[subset].  
Create an new actor *reference and name it something like actor scratch or  
temp actor ref.  
Add a new set *reference command within the loop value and set the *actor  
scratch reference to the ^[subset] oﬀset by the ^loop index.  
6 of 20  
01/11/2016 03:27 PM  
Ch. 7: Advanced Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._7:_Advanced_Scri...  
NOTE: Remember, to get the oﬀset by to appear, allowing you to index in to a  
set, you should point the to of the set *reference at the ^[subset]'s <self>.  
Move the ﬁnd variable command out of the for each and into the loop value  
block, and set the owned by parameter to the *actor scratch reference.  
Delete the now unneeded for each command. The ﬁnished block of script should  
look as follows.  
You're now doing the same thing the for each was doing but with a signiﬁcantly faster  
loop value. The speed enhancement will only get better the bigger your set is, since for  
each would have to copy an even bigger set over. Loop value's loop index also opens up  
some interesting possibilities and can be valuable for many operations, if you actually did  
want to assign an index to each object, for example.  
As a note, several commands support the idea of indexing in like the set *reference  
command. Find variable happens to be one of them, so if you wanted to be incredibly  
optimized, you could even get away with the following.  
7 of 20  
01/11/2016 03:27 PM  
Ch. 7: Advanced Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._7:_Advanced_Scri...  
My scripts won't be doing this, since I think it's important to show the usual use case  
where you need a reference to index in, but keep in mind that there are shortcuts out  
there!  
Looping Backwards  
When you use for each, remember, a whole copy of the set is kept so the for each can  
operate on it. Loop value leaves us to our own devices, however - if we modify the [set]  
we're looping on while we loop on it, we could have problems! For example, just imagine  
a loop where we are excluding members from the set while we go. The item at index 0  
could disappear, and then when the loop goes to index 1, the old item at index 1 will be at  
index 0. When we loop over a set where we may be removing members as we go, it is  
best to loop backwards. This can be accomplished by using the loop index to count  
backwards. Let's change the Enemy Manager's script to use loop value and loop  
backwards.  
Open the Enemy Manager script, and, above the for each line, add a new loop  
value command and loop over the *[enemies ref] count.  
Create a new value variable and name it something like loop scratch or loop max  
index.  
Before the loop value, set the loop scratch to the *[enemies ref] count - 1.  
8 of 20  
01/11/2016 03:27 PM  
Ch. 7: Advanced Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._7:_Advanced_Scri...  
We'll be using loop scratch as the index of the last member of the set (the count minus  
one), then, when we set our reference, we can index in based on the end of the set minus  
the loop index.  
Create a new actor *reference and name it something like actor scratch or temp  
actor ref.  
Add a new set *reference command within the loop value, and, similar to before,  
set it to index in to the *[enemies ref] set, but change the oﬀset to loop scratch -  
^loop index.  
Just to clarify what's happening here, imagine the enemies set has a full 3 members in it.  
As we run through the set 3 times, ^loop index will be 0, 1, and 2, respectively. By  
setting the loop scratch value to the set count minus 1, it will be 2. Then, we we loop  
through, the oﬀset will be 2 minus 0, 2 minus 1, and 2 minus 2; respectively, 2, 1, and 0.  
Move the control command from the for each block into the loop value block, and  
change it to control *actor scratch.  
9 of 20  
01/11/2016 03:27 PM  
Ch. 7: Advanced Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._7:_Advanced_Scri...  
Note that the set [set] line also referred to ^current in [for each] and broke. Be  
sure to change it to exclude *actor scratch.  
Finally, delete the unneeded for each line. The ﬁnal check enemy behavior should  
look as follows:  
10 of 20  
01/11/2016 03:27 PM  
Ch. 7: Advanced Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._7:_Advanced_Scri...  
Now we can use loop value to iterate over the *[enemies ref] set backwards, which  
allows us to exclude members from the set as we go. Even if we excluded every member,  
the oﬀset (loop scratch minus ^loop index) will always be pointing to something valid  
since we start at the end.  
Blocks  
So far, we have been able to use the comment command to add comments to our scripts  
and place them strategically. There is another kind of comment, called a block, which is  
a comment that comes with an indentation. It serves the purpose of a comment as well as  
provides an indent. The uses of the indent will become apparent later, but, for now, let's  
add a couple blocks to increase the readability of our scripts.  
Open the Enemy Manager's script and add a new block command at the top of the  
check enemy behavior. In the text ﬁeld that appears, enter something like  
checking for removed enemies.  
Select the following four lines and drag them into the block.  
11 of 20  
01/11/2016 03:27 PM  
Ch. 7: Advanced Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._7:_Advanced_Scri...  
Collapse the block with the minus symbol and create another block command,  
entitling it something like check if we should spawn more enemies.  
Drag the check value block into the block.  
Now you have two distinct blocks of script that you can easily collapse when you're not  
working on them. This is tremendously helpful once you start editing very long or  
complex scripts. Additionally, being able to put a comment on a block allows a newcomer  
(or your forgetful self after not looking at the script for a month) to understand where to  
start looking for certain things.  
Flow Outs  
One of the aforementioned beneﬁts of the block command is the capacity to add  
indentations at will. Beyond letting your organize scripts, indentations open up the  
possibility of more condensed and legible scripting via ﬂow outs. Most lines come in  
with ﬂow continue on them, and we've used the else and else if to add variations on the  
ﬂow, along with the ﬂow end which stops the script from executing any further. Flow out  
is another special ﬂow, similar to end, but it lets you ﬂow out of indentations any number  
12 of 20  
01/11/2016 03:27 PM  
Ch. 7: Advanced Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._7:_Advanced_Scri...  
of steps. If I could have you master only one thing besides ﬁnd subset, I'd have it be  
ﬂow out, as it's similarly powerful and full of potential.  
Imagine that ﬂow continue actually means ﬂow out 1 step.  
When the script reaches a ﬂow continue, it has nowhere to go down in the script, but, the  
script does have somewhere to go by going to the left by one indentation - then it can go  
down. If you pull open a ﬂow continue parameter, you'll note several outs.  
Try changing a ﬂow continue to a ﬂow out 2.  
Note the arrow that appears (there is a display bug in the current tool where you may  
need to click somewhere to get it to show after picking the ﬂow out). Now, instead of  
doing a ﬂow out 1 (a continue) by stepping out 1 indentation, the script will ﬂow out 2  
and step out 2 indentations.  
Why do this? In general, it has three primary uses:  
ﬂowing out of a loop.  
ﬂowing out to create an inverse check value, when you want to have a check value  
decide not to do something.  
ﬂowing out in multiple, diﬀerent cases to create a sort of OR statement.  
Flowing Out of a Loop  
13 of 20  
01/11/2016 03:27 PM  
Ch. 7: Advanced Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._7:_Advanced_Scri...  
Imagine, for a moment, that we only want the loop value in the Enemy Manager to  
remove one actor at a time from the *[enemies set]. When the control fails, we could  
change the ﬂow continue to a ﬂow out 2 to exit to loop.  
Open the Enemy Manager's script and change the ﬂow continue after the set [set]  
line in the loop to a ﬂow out 2.  
Now, as soon as the control failed, the ﬂow out 2 would take the script out of the loop by  
bypassing the ﬂow loop value. Though it was done here just for the sake of  
demonstration, this sort of usage is critical when we're doing a lookup in a set and trying  
to ﬁnd a particular member. Once we ﬁnd it, we don't want to look through the rest of the  
set - we've found what we're looking for and want to exit to loop. We'll do something like  
this once we get to structures.  
Be sure to undo the change you did - it was just a demo!  
Flowing Out as a Not  
In some cases, you may have several, nested layers of check values (or other checks)  
where you are trying to decide if you should do something. Sometimes, it can get very  
hard to read when you're trying to check if several things are true, which creates several  
indentations, and then ﬁnally doing something way down deep in there. Take this made  
up script, for example:  
Imagine the DO SOMETHING comment actually was several lines. It can be bothersome  
to read and work on scripts when they get that deep, horizontally. This same construct  
could be expressed in the following way with ﬂow outs by reversing each check and  
14 of 20  
01/11/2016 03:27 PM  
Ch. 7: Advanced Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._7:_Advanced_Scri...  
ﬂowing out if it succeeds.  
While those uneducated about ﬂow out may quail at this example, I ﬁnd it much easier  
to read. The plain english of the ﬁrst example is: If this and that and something, etc.  
are all true, then do something. In the second example, it's: If this, then do something  
unless either that, something, something else, etc. are not true.  
By avoiding the unnecessary indentations we can keep our script more legible (in my  
opinion) and make adding to it easier. In the ﬁrst example, if you wanted to add another  
check, you'd have to make a new check and then drag the whole do something block  
into it. In the second example, all you do is add another negative check, ﬂow out, and  
you're done. No messing around with the actual script that does something.  
Flowing Out as an Or  
Unlike the above, imaginary examples, we actually have an application for this concept.  
This may be the most challenging of the ﬂow out uses to grasp, but it is also the most  
powerful. In some cases you will want to write an OR statement, meaning that in any  
number of disparate cases, you'd like the same thing to happen. The Player Projectile is a  
perfect example: in three diﬀerent cases, we want the projectile to run the remove  
myself command.  
15 of 20  
01/11/2016 03:27 PM  
Ch. 7: Advanced Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._7:_Advanced_Scri...  
Though the remove myself command is just a single line, imagine if you had several  
lines you wanted to run in each case. You'd have to duplicate those lines in each instance  
- it would be a waste of space and a pain to maintain, as if you changed something in one  
location, you'd need to change it in all of them. With clever use of ﬂow outs, you can  
have the script ﬂow in a way that allows you to have only one remove line which gets run  
in any of the cases.  
Open the Player Projectile's script and add a new block command above all your  
checks - you may leave the text ﬁeld blank.  
Add another block command inside the block you just made. Adjust its ﬂow  
continue to be a ﬂow out 2.  
Now, this is an interesting construct. Everything that you place below the ﬂow out 2 will  
actually never be executed! The ﬂow out 2 in the 2nd block will take the script beyond  
whatever you place below it.  
Add a new remove command below the ﬂow out 2 and have it remove myself.  
16 of 20  
01/11/2016 03:27 PM  
Ch. 7: Advanced Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._7:_Advanced_Scri...  
Drag each one of the three check value or ﬁnd subset blocks into the 2nd block.  
In the ﬁrst (check value distance from my origin) block, change the ﬂow continue  
below the remove command to a ﬂow out 2.  
17 of 20  
01/11/2016 03:27 PM  
Ch. 7: Advanced Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._7:_Advanced_Scri...  
In the second (ﬁnd subset of Enemy Actors) block, do the same with the ﬂow  
continue below the remove command.  
In the third (check value my separation from my ground) block, do the same with  
its ﬂow continue below the remove command, except you'll need to use a ﬂow out  
18 of 20  
01/11/2016 03:27 PM  
Ch. 7: Advanced Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._7:_Advanced_Scri...  
3 since it is more deeply indented.  
Finally, delete all the three, old remove commands, leaving only the one below the  
block.  
Bam! Yes, that deserved a bam. How's that for eﬀiciency? Not only is our script using less  
lines, but it's even easier to maintain. If we need to add additional things to happen  
19 of 20  
01/11/2016 03:27 PM  
Ch. 7: Advanced Scripting - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._7:_Advanced_Scri...  
beyond the remove myself command, we can add it very simply in one place. If we need  
to add additional conditions when the projectile should remove itself, like checking for  
walls or time, it's just a matter of adding another check to the inner block.  
It may seem daunting to read at ﬁrst, but, once you get used to it, I actually ﬁnd it easier  
to read and maintain. The statement clearly reads, to me: if the projectile is too far from  
its origin, or it hits an enemy, or it hits the ground, then go away.  
Now that you are a complete scripting expert, you're ready to learn about the awesome  
power of macros and structures in the next chapter, Macros and Structures.
